skills_i_can_now_do: Write a counterclaim-then-claim sentence using the 'Although X, Y because Z' structure
Concede the other side's strongest point while still holding my position
Make sure the reason Z actually answers the conceded objection X, not just adds a random pro-side reason
Spot fake concessions (e.g., an 'although' that compliments your own side or never takes a position)
Diagnose a weak claim that lacks a concession and identify the fix
Run the 3-question checklist (concedes? holds position? reason answers objection?) on my own sentence
Identify the strongest objection myself on a new topic (daylight saving) and write a claim that answers it
terms_learned: Although X, Y because Z frame

skills_i_can_now_do: Concede a real objection and still hold a clear position (concede-and-hold) instead of collapsing into 'both sides are right'
Recognize and name 'concede-and-collapse' — a claim that concedes but then drifts into no position
Pick out which sentence concedes AND holds among options that collapse or shrug
Distinguish a real concession from a fake one (an 'although' that helps my own side or concedes something nobody disputes)
Diagnose a collapsing claim and fix it by holding a clear position with a reason after conceding
Write a concede-and-hold claim with the frame, then without the frame, and pass the 3-question check
Transfer the concede-and-hold move to a brand-new topic (longer school year) and check it myself

felt_repeated.what: Nearly the whole lesson re-teaches lesson 1 move for move: the 3-question checklist (with today's version actually easier — only asking if a reason exists, not whether it answers the objection), the fake-concession trap question, the diagnose-the-weak-claim exercise, and even the same daylight saving topic reused. Steps 7–9 were three versions of the same sentence. The only genuinely new things we

skills_i_can_now_do: Stretch the concede-then-answer move from a single sentence into a full counterargument paragraph
Use a specific fact from a source as the ANSWER to an objection instead of just a reason
Run the 3-part POSITION / CONCEDE / ANSWER check on a full paragraph
Pick out the option that concedes AND answers the objection (vs. naming-and-dropping or a random pro-side pivot) in multiple choice
Diagnose a weak paragraph where the answer just restates the claim and identify the fix (add fact + reason that answers the objection)
Write a concede + answer pair onto a given position using source evidence (school year cost objection)
Write a fresh concede-then-answer on a different objection (summer jobs) and stay inside the objection's frame instead of dodging
Write a full counterargument paragraph (position + concede + answer) that answers cost with cost using source facts
Transfer the counterargument paragraph move to a brand-new topic (congestion pricing) with source facts, answering a fairness objection on fairness terms
Extract usable facts and identify the strongest objection on each side from a new pair of source articles
Turn the critics' own admission against them as an answering move
terms_learned: naming the objection vs. answering it
where_i_struggled: Left the POSITION implied instead of stated outright in the Step 7 fresh concede-then-answer; caught it myself during the check and noted the fix

felt_repeated.what: The first half of the teach section re-explained the concede-and-answer / 'does Z answer X?' move already mastered in g10_l02 (and the Z-check from g10_l01); the POSITION/CONCEDE/ANSWER check is the old 3-question checklist with new labels; the longer-school-year topic was literally the transfer topic in lesson 2; Steps 4-5 were two multiple-choice checks on a distinction already mastered; and Ste

skills_i_can_now_do: Write an analytical claim about literature that names an author's choice AND its purpose, not just plot summary
Distinguish analysis from summary and from mere opinion ('the story is good')
Distinguish two different failure modes: reporting a choice without a purpose vs. stating a theme without naming a specific craft choice
Run the craft check (choice named? purpose named? about the author's doing, not plot?) on my own analytical claims
Diagnose a weak analytical draft that only reports a choice happens and rewrite it to name what the choice is doing
Write an analytical claim cold on a choice not pre-analyzed for me (front-loading the heart trouble in the opening line)
Transfer the analytical claim move to a new informational text, picking my own structural choice (small-vs-large contrasts in the highway article)

felt_repeated.what: The core content is new (literary analysis, not argument), but the lesson reuses the exact same skeleton as lessons 1–3 (teach the move → worked example → three MC checks → write with frame → fix weak draft → write cold → transfer), and the craft check is basically the same idea as the 3-question checklists from lessons 2 and 3, just aimed at author choices. Also internal padding: Step 4's correct

skills_i_can_now_do: Distinguish a craft claim (technique + effect) from a content claim (just what happens or how a character feels)
Spot the disguised-content trap: an author verb like 'Chopin shows/reveals' in front of a feeling is still a content claim
Recognize that quoting a word from the text doesn't make a claim a craft claim unless it says what the word's placement does
Diagnose the subtler middle-draft failure: naming a technique but making the effect still just a feeling
Pick out the true craft claim among dressed-up content options in multiple choice (went 3-for-3 first try)
Write a craft claim with the 'Chopin ___ so ___' frame and pass the craft test (technique? effect on reader? not just a feeling?)
Fix a weak content claim by naming a technique (limited point of view), which forces adding a reader effect
Write a craft claim cold, without the frame, on a self-chosen technique (irony) and pass the self-check
Transfer craft claims to an informational/nonfiction text (structural placement of the contamination section) and pass the craft test
Choose a different textual move than the lesson's example instead of copying it
terms_learned: craft test (technique? effect on reader? just a feeling?)

felt_repeated.what: The core technique-plus-effect requirement and the 'choice without a purpose' diagnosis are straight out of Lesson 4; 'craft vs. content claim' is the same analysis-vs-summary distinction with new labels. Also flagged padding: three multiple-choice checks in a row on the same distinction, a Step 8 diagnosis the lesson pre-answered for me, and a Step 7 frame that felt like training wheels I didn't

skills_i_can_now_do: Name a rhetorical device AND state what it does to the reader (device-to-effect), instead of stopping at the label
Recognize that praising the craft ('look how detailed this is') is still label-only, not analysis
Distinguish an effect on the reader from an effect on a character when evaluating device-to-effect claims
Pick out the option that states a real reader effect vs. label-only, craft-compliment, or character-effect traps in multiple choice
Diagnose a label-only claim ('Chopin uses irony') and identify the fix: state what the device does to the reader
Write a device-to-effect sentence with the frame and pass the device-to-effect check
Rewrite a weak label-only draft by adding a 'so' clause naming the effect on the reader, and identify which check question the fix answered
Write a device-to-effect sentence cold, without the frame, and self-check it
Transfer the device-to-effect move to a nonfiction passage, analyzing a structural choice (ordering) and its effect on the reader
terms_learned: effect on the reader vs. effect on the character

felt_repeated.what: The core 'name the choice + say what it does to the reader' move is a rebrand of lesson 5's choice-without-purpose / analysis-vs-summary work (and lesson 4 covered analysis-vs-summary too); the device-to-effect test reuses the 3-question checklist template from lessons 1-3; the Step 10-11 transfer-to-new-topic trick mirrors lesson 3's congestion pricing transfer. Within the lesson: Steps 4-6 were

skills_i_can_now_do: Extend a device-to-effect sentence with a warrant — a 'that matters because' that ties the effect to the author's purpose
Distinguish a real warrant from a 'because' that only restates or explains the effect (backward-pointing 'because')
Recognize the praise trap: 'that is an interesting/nice line' is not a warrant or an effect
Run the Device / Effect / Warrant three-question check on a sentence or draft
Pick out in multiple choice the option that reaches the author's purpose vs. thicker effect description or a restated effect
Diagnose a draft that stops at effect and identify that it's missing a warrant
Write a full device-effect-warrant claim with the frame, using source facts, and pass the check
Fix a weak praise-only draft by rewriting it with a real effect and an explicit warrant stated outright ('that matters because')
Choose my own device (the number pairing of expectation vs. reality) and write a significance claim cold, without the frame
Extract usable facts from a source article and identify the author's stated purpose to use as the warrant

felt_repeated.what: Student said the lesson's core is Lesson 5's 'purpose' idea stapled to Lesson 6's 'effect' idea (g10_l06_device_to_effect); also flagged the same model sentence appearing three times (Steps 1, 3, and as MC answer C), which made the checks 'almost impossible to miss,' and noted the Device/Effect/Warrant checklist reuses the same 3-question skeleton from Lessons 1–3.

skills_i_can_now_do: Chain two devices/choices that build the same point instead of relying on a single-shot observation (choice → purpose, extended from device_to_effect)

Pick the multiple-choice option that links two choices to one shared point (vs. one-choice-plus-praise or 'separate way' options)
Diagnose a weak draft as a single-shot (one choice, vague effect) needing a second choice building the same point
Run the 3-question chain check (two choices? same point? point named?) on my own writing
Write a device-chain analytical sentence with the frame, deliberately choosing fresh choices instead of copying the worked example
Fix a single-shot weak draft by adding a second choice and naming the shared point
Write a cold device-chain paragraph on the same text without the frame
Transfer the device-chain paragraph to a brand-new text (weather source), deliberately choosing a different chain than the example given
terms_learned: stacking trap (two choices working separately, no shared point)

Steps 6–8 were the same task three times on one text; multiple-choice answers were pre-spoiled by the wor

skills_i_can_now_do: Write an analysis paragraph with CHOICE / EFFECT / WARRANT labeled parts, revising a summary paragraph into analysis
Use the term 'warrant' for the why-it-matters sentence (same move as 'significance' from Lesson 7)
Run the 3-question analysis check (choice? effect? warrant?) on my own paragraph
Predict the score of a paragraph (retelling vs. analysis) and be right on the reveal
Pick out the analytical option vs. the retelling option in multiple choice

Revise a summary paragraph into full analysis cold, without the checklist in front of me
Transfer the choice-effect-warrant revision to a brand-new text (wetlands), picking my own choice instead of the one the prompt handed me

terms_learned: warrant (new word for the 'why it matters' / significance sentence)
score prediction (predict the score, see the reveal)

felt_repeated.what: The core idea (analysis vs. summary) is the same as Lesson 4, and the choice+effect+warrant move is Lessons 6, 7, and 8 combined — 'warrant' is just a new word for Lesson 7's 'significance.' The 3-question check mirrors Lesson 2's checklist, the diagnose step is Lesson 5's content-vs-craft distinction 'wearing a hat,' Step 8 is Step 7 again, and the multiple-choice reveal reused the Step 3 AFTER p

skills_i_can_now_do: Revise a paragraph by the add/delete rule: every sentence must serve the point — cut what's off-purpose even if it's true, add what's needed
Identify which sentence to cut because it doesn't serve the paragraph's stated point (e.g., loss-of-land sentence in a wildlife paragraph)
Identify which sentence to add when the point needs a HOW/mechanism (e.g., plants and soft ground absorbing wave force for storm protection)
Diagnose the off-purpose sentence in a draft and reject fixes that pad or merely move it instead of cutting it
Revise a draft with the frame: delete the off-purpose sentence and strengthen the point with a specific source fact, then run the 3-question check
Check and fix a weak draft: cut the off-purpose sentence, explain why it doesn't serve the point, and add a stat that strengthens it
Revise on my own without a frame: cut the off-purpose sentence and add a mechanism detail so every sentence serves the cleaning-water point
Transfer the add/delete revision move to a brand-new topic (interstate system) with no frame: cut off-purpose funding history and add a HOW with source facts
Extract usable key facts from new source articles (wetlands and interstate sources) to bank for revision
terms_learned: every sentence should serve the point
open_questions: Can I handle a harder version where the off-purpose sentence is adjacent/related to the point (e.g., a flood-storage stat in a wildlife paragraph) so the cut actually requires judgment? All the practice items here were obvious calls.

skills_i_can_now_do: Reorder scrambled sentences into a logical sequence (chronological/earliest-to-latest) so each sentence builds on the last
Reorder sentences by cause-to-effect order (e.g., trucks pound pavement → pavement cracks → crews repave)
Spot that signpost words like 'First/Next/Finally' stapled onto a backwards order don't make the order logical — labels can lie
Diagnose whether a weak draft's problem is sentence order vs. other problems (added content, style fixes)
Run the 3-question sequence check (each sentence builds on the last, no jumping back) on my own paragraph
Reorder sentences without a frame, tracking how each sentence's subject picks up where the last left off
Transfer the reorder-for-logical-sequence move to a brand-new topic (recycling process: collect → sort → manufacture) with no frame, matching the source's process order
terms_learned: the trap of leaving sentences in the order they occurred to you
signpost words (First/Next/Finally) that can disguise a backwards order
open_questions: Can I handle a scrambled paragraph of six or seven sentences where two orderings both seem defensible and I have to argue for one? Every item here had one obviously correct chronological/causal order.
Can I handle a draft where the order problem is buried in the middle of otherwise fine sentences, or a sentence (like a background fact) that could plausibly go in two places?

skills_i_can_now_do: Judge a reason on its content, not its feeling — spot a circular reason (e.g., 'important because it matters') where the reason just restates the claim
Run the three-question check on a reason (reason not feeling? specific and checkable? circular?)
Pick the stronger of two 'twin' sentences that differ only in their reason, choosing the one with a specific, checkable reason over a circular one
Recognize that confident/persuasive tone words ('a smart, responsible choice') are just judgment restated, not a real reason
Identify the correct fix for a circular reason (replace it with a checkable reason) versus non-fixes like making the sentence longer, bolder, or shorter
Score a sentence's reason and diagnose circularity ('good for the environment' ≈ 'saves resources')
Rewrite a weak twin using the frame, replacing a circular reason with a specific source fact (152 million Btu / 1,024 gallons of gas for a ton of aluminum cans)
Write my own claim with a specific, checkable reason from source evidence (EPA's 2012 finding of ~681,000 recycling jobs) and run the check on it
Improve a weak draft cold (no frame) by adding specific, checkable source facts (94 million tons kept out of landfills in 2018)
Transfer the circular-reason fix to a brand-new topic (weather forecasts), replacing 'because they are important' with a concrete, source-based effect (warnings reach phones/radio/TV in seconds)
Extract usable numbers/facts from a source in anticipation of needing them for rewrite steps
Use the 'twins that differ on ONE move' framing to think about revision comparisons
terms_learned: strong/weak twin (twins that differ on ONE move)
open_questions: Can I handle a hard twin comparison where BOTH reasons are specific and checkable and I have to judge which one better supports the claim?
Can I catch a reason that sounds specific ('uses advanced sorting technology') but is actually unverifiable fluff — one that requires judgment, not pattern-matching?

felt_repeated.what: The core skill — spotting a reason that just restates itself ('important because it matters') and demanding a specific, checkable 'because' — is the same territory as lesson 9 (analysis_check) and overlaps with lesson 5 (claim_craft_vs_content). The three-question check is a compressed version of checks from lessons 2, 3, and 9; the longer/bolder distractors repeat the wrong-answer pattern from le

skills_i_can_now_do: Name the error type (off-purpose / out of order / circular reason / too general) as a label before fixing, so the type names the fix
Diagnose 'too general' in multiple choice by checking that nothing specific is named ('science' and 'help people' name nothing)
Distinguish a circular reason from 'too general' when the draft names a real tool (radar) but the reason still restates the claim
Spot an off-purpose sentence (history trivia) inside an otherwise process-focused draft and name it as the type
Diagnose 'too general' and rewrite with specific source details (radiosondes, ~160 NEXRAD radars, warnings reaching phones in seconds)
Deliberately build a flawed draft (out-of-order steps) and then diagnose and fix it by reordering into the logical observe → model → adjust sequence
Cold-diagnose a messy draft with multiple flaws, pick the clearest fixable type (off-purpose), and rewrite fixing more than one problem
Transfer the diagnose-and-fix move to a new topic (wetlands), name a circular reason, and rewrite with measurable source facts
Use the four error-type labels as a revision framework for my own essays
terms_learned: name the type → the type names the fix
where_i_struggled: Nothing really hard — every item was the obvious single-flaw version; only Step 9's messy multi-flaw draft required real judgment, and I only got one taste of that
Less sure about diagnosing drafts where multiple error types are tangled together and I have to decide which to fix first (self-rated 0.7)
open_questions: Can I diagnose a draft with TWO real error types where I have to decide which one to fix first?
Can I handle a sentence that's arguably on-purpose, where deciding whether to cut it takes actual revision judgment?

felt_repeated.what: Three of the four error types are straight reruns: 'off-purpose, cut it' is lesson 10 (add/delete), 'out of order, reorder' is lesson 11 (logical sequence), and 'circular/too general' overlaps lesson 12 (strong vs. weak twins) and lesson 9's analysis check. Step 6 was the exact task from lesson 10 relabeled — and still the obvious version I already said was too easy. Step 7 even reused the same dr

skills_i_can_now_do: Combine add, delete, and reorder in a single revision pass instead of doing each move separately
Recognize the trap of fixing the obvious flaw and stopping — keep revising until all three checks pass
Run the 3-question revision check (serve purpose? missing detail? order builds?) on a paragraph
Pick the revision option that does all three moves on the right material vs. surface polish or deleting the actual point (multiple choice)
Diagnose which moves a draft needs (cut off-purpose line, confirm/fix causal order) and identify the correct fix
Score a revised draft by checking whether the required moves actually happened (spot a draft that kept the off-purpose lines and shortened the key detail)
Execute a guided revision (delete specified lines, add sponge mechanism + 60→12 stat, put the point first) on the flood-protection paragraph
Write a fresh purpose-driven paragraph (wildlife shelter) with a point-first order and specific supporting stats, then self-check it
Revise a draft cold with no checklist: cut off-purpose lines, add mechanism details, and reorder into causal order (cleaning water)
Cut an adjacent-but-off-purpose sentence ('recycling has grown over the years') where the sentence is on-topic but doesn't serve the paragraph's purpose
Transfer the combined revise-for-purpose move to a brand-new topic (recycling/materials recovery) with source facts, ordering the process as it actually happens
Choose stronger evidence than the minimum asked for (adding the 60→12 stat beyond the required sponge detail)
where_i_struggled: Step 5's 'reorder' felt half-invented — the draft was already in causal order, so I had to get the answer partly by elimination
Step 8's self-check felt hollow: running a delete/reorder check on a paragraph I just wrote on purpose almost guarantees a clean pass, so I didn't really test the skill on a messy draft
open_questions: Can I handle a genuinely messy multi-flaw draft where the combined moves interact and I have to decide what to fix first, rather than three obvious flaws stapled together?
Can I handle more adjacent-but-off-purpose cuts that require real judgment (like the 'recycling has grown' line), since I only got one of those?
Does the 3-question check actually add value on fresh writing, or is it only useful on drafts someone else wrote?

felt_repeated.what: The lesson's whole pitch is combining moves already taught: add/delete came from g10_l10_add_delete and ordering from g10_l11_logical_sequence; the 3-question check is just those lessons' checks stacked into one list. skills_i_can_now_do: Map an evidence pool: check every source, name what each one lets me say, and name the objection I'll answer
Follow the 5-step pool-mapping order (READ, LABEL, DECIDE, PICK, THEN plan)
Pick out the plan that labels both sources and uses both (vs. grabbing one source or hoarding quotes without assigning jobs) in multiple choice
Diagnose a one-source-only draft (con-source-only or pro-source-only) as the core pool-mapping mistake
Write a pool map for a source set with and without the frame, and pass the 3-question pool check
Write a pool map cold on a new source set (daylight saving articles)
Pick the strongest objection from a con source that raises several objections, instead of dumping them all

open_questions: Can I map a pool where the sources are messier — where it's genuinely unclear what job each source should have, instead of a clean pro/con pair?

felt_repeated.what: The lesson's core move — extracting usable facts from sources and naming the strongest objection — is exactly what I already did in g10_l14; this lesson just named it 'map the pool.' It even reused the SAME congestion pricing articles from the earlier counterargument-paragraph work, so I mapped a pool I'd already mapped. Steps 5 and 6 were two multiple-choice checks on the same 'use both sources'

skills_i_can_now_do: Define a synthesis claim: my own position that draws on more than one source instead of reporting one of them
Distinguish a synthesis claim from a source report (spot the giveaway when a sentence's subject is 'Source X says/explains/suggests')
Recognize that summarizing BOTH sources is still summarizing — 'reports both sources' ≠ 'draws on both sources' because no side is taken
Diagnose why a summary-style thesis fails and identify the fix that produces a stance
Write a synthesis claim with the frame (position + multi-source reasons + reframed objection) and pass the 3-question check (my position? draws on the set? arguable?)
Write a fresh synthesis claim from a different angle (state-choice angle) and audit it against the checklist
Write a synthesis claim cold, no frame, built from the point where the two sources overlap (the switching itself is the harm)
Find the position two sources jointly build (their overlap) rather than just picking one side and conceding to the other
Transfer the synthesis claim move to a second source set (longer school year), taking the pro side's problem and the con side's solution seriously
terms_learned: source report (naming a source as the subject of the sentence)
reports both sources vs. draws on both sources
where_i_struggled: Nothing was hard — the skill clicked in about ten seconds because it's ~80% the concede-and-hold move I already have at 0.9 confidence
Step 7's frame basically forced me to reproduce the Step 4 worked example with the source's facts swapped in — not much thinking required
Step 8's self-check felt hollow again: running a checklist on a claim I just wrote to pass the checklist almost guarantees a pass (same complaint I logged in lessons 10 and 15)
Steps 7, 8, and 9 were three consecutive reps of the identical task on the identical sources — by Step 9 I was producing, not learning
The sources were reused from earlier lessons (daylight saving pool from lesson 15, school year topic from lessons 13–14), so I was never tested on reading NEW material cold
open_questions: Can I write a synthesis claim on a source set I've never seen before, reading new material cold instead of reusing pools I already mapped?
Would my synthesis check mean anything if I had to audit a messy claim written by someone else, or write fast without the checklist and THEN audit it?
Is 'synthesis claim' actually a new skill, or just concede-and-hold plus the rule that the claim must touch both sources?

felt_repeated.what: The synthesis claim is basically the same shape as concede-and-hold from lessons 1–3 — the worked example IS a concede-then-answer move, and the check tool reuses lesson 2's 3-question checklist structure with different questions plugged in. On top of that, the daylight saving sources are the exact set from g10_l15_evidence_pool, and the longer school year set repeats the topic from g10_l14_revise

skills_i_can_now_do: Weave facts from two different sources into one sentence that lands on a single claim, instead of listing 'Source 1 says X, Source 2 says Y'
Run the 3-question weave check (same point? connected? one claim?) on a cross-source point
Pick out the truly woven option vs. a list, a single-source point, or two-points-stapled-together in multiple choice (including the sneaky 'two separate reasons support it' fake)
Diagnose a book-report list draft and identify the fix (connect the facts so they meet on one claim)
Write a woven cross-source point with the frame, choosing facts that collide rather than just cooperate (equity argument cuts both ways)
Write a fresh woven point without the frame and run the check on it (targeted summer programs beat a universal longer year)
where_i_struggled: Nothing was actually hard — Steps 5 and 7 were obvious single-flaw versions of the same distinction; only Step 6's sneaky 'two separate reasons' option made me pause even two seconds

Step 9's self-check felt hollow again: running the weave check on a point I just built to pass the check almost guarantees a pass — same complaint I made in lessons 10 and 15

felt_repeated.what: The core move — making a fact from one place meet a point from another so both land on one claim — is the same muscle as lesson 3's 'answer cost with cost using source facts'; this lesson just names it 'weaving' and applies it to two sources. skills_i_can_now_do: Name the relationship between two sources: where they agree, where they clash, and pinpoint the real disagreement underneath
Recognize that two sources can agree on the facts and differ only on what to do about them, and name that as the insight
Spot fake relationship statements (list-plus-'disagree' with nothing specific, or comparing surface features like length) in multiple choice
Diagnose a vague relationship sentence ('they have different views') and identify the fix: name the exact agreement and clash
Write a relationship sentence with the frame that names agreement, clash, and the real underlying issue — going deeper than the model answer (sequencing/trust rather than surface fairness)
Write a fresh relationship sentence from a new angle on the same source set and pass the agree/clash/real-issue check
Write a relationship sentence without the frame
Pin down a non-obvious clash on a new source set (daylight saving) where the disagreement isn't clean pro/con but 'which fix and who decides'
Name the relationship on a new source set: agreement, clash, and the real disagreement (who gets to choose the trade-off)
terms_learned: agreement vs. clash (naming the relationship between sources)
sources agreeing on facts but differing on what to do about them

Steps 5–7 were three multiple-choice checks in a row on the identical distinction; I'd have been fine with one
Both source sets were reused topics (congestion pricing from earlier, daylight saving from lessons 7 and 15), so this didn't test whether I can find a relationship cold
Step 9's self-check felt hollow again — same issue I flagged in lessons 10 and 15: checking a sentence I just wrote on purpose to pass the checklist almost guarantees a clean pass
The only step that took real reading was pinning down the daylight saving clash, since the critic wasn't defending the switch but questioning which fix and who decides
open_questions: Can I find and name a source relationship cold, on a topic and sources I haven't already worked with in earlier lessons?
Would running the relationship checklist on someone else's messy draft with a subtle failure test more than checking my own on-purpose sentence?
Can I handle source pairs where the clash isn't a clean pro/con, more like the daylight saving 'which fix and who decides' case?

felt_repeated.what: The setup felt familiar: lesson 17 (cross_source) already had me reading a pro/con pair and pulling objections from each side, and lesson 16 (synthesis_claim) was about combining sources into one argument — this is just the 'name the relationship' piece. On top of that, the congestion pricing sources repeat lesson 14's topic, daylight saving repeats lesson 7 (and the evidence pool lesson), the wit

skills_i_can_now_do: Tell a woven cross-source paragraph apart from a source-by-source list
Apply the delete-test: cut one source and check whether the point collapses
Diagnose why a paragraph is source-by-source (separate blocks, no shared point) and reject wrong fixes like 'just add length'
Revise a source-by-source paragraph into a woven one using the frame, connecting both sources in the same sentences around one shared point
Choose a different shared point than the worked example instead of copying its AFTER paragraph
Write a fresh woven paragraph from scratch (watch/warning urgency-scale point) and pass the three-part check
Revise into a woven paragraph without the frame (flood tracking-to-warning causal chain)
Transfer the weaving move to a brand-new topic (recycling sorting-to-remaking chain) with source facts and pass the check
